utterances: Masood says Musharraf is also being blamed for other problems beyond the political turmoil such as severe shortages of basic necessities.
They are not available or they are available at a price which is unacceptable.
Like, for instance, I mean, you know, food stuff - ordinary wheat and rice and, you know, cereals.
And there are widespread electricity blackouts.
Some rural areas are without power for up to 16 hours a day.
Masood says those real, practical hardships will likely increase the antipathy toward Musharraf, who does not plan to return to Pakistan for another week.
Jackie Northam, NPR News, Islamabad.
This is ALL THINGS CONSIDERED from NPR News.
This is Debbie Elliott.
There was more reaction today to the recommendations of the bipartisan Iraq Study Group.
Iraq's President Jalal Talabani blasted the panel's report, calling it unjust and dangerous to the sovereignty of his country.
Talabani, an Iraqi Kurd, has his own reasons for disliking the panel's recommendations.
But his rejection puts him in the same camp as some powerful Shiite leaders.
The Iraqi president made his statement on a day when the sectarian violence in Iraq seemed to take on stronger overtones of ethnic cleansing and more organized fighting.
NPR's Corey Flintoff reports from Baghdad.
President Talabani appeared on Iraqi television to denounce the study group's report in no uncertain terms.
(Through translator) I considered the Baker-Hamilton report to be an insult to the Iraqi people.
The commission doesn't have the right to threaten us.
We are an independent country.

Yes, two years ago I undertook a trip to prepare a plan for bringing 2,000 young men from the Taliban movement into a sort of a life skills training course as a platform for them getting, you know, peaceful jobs.
I presented that to the provincial authorities in Helmand.
I was thrown out of the country for my pains.

Two years later I have the pleasure of listening to the president of Afghanistan proposing to do more or less the same thing on a slightly grander scale, and with a much larger budget than I ever anticipated.
So times have changed.

As we just heard Secretary Clinton making a comparison to the kind of efforts in Iraq, one thing that did work in Iraq was entire tribes, Sunni tribes, were brought around, but could that work in Afghanistan, or could that work partly in Afghanistan?
I think it's going to work to the extent that young men who are members of the Taliban movement are also members of the tribe.
But in the areas where the insurgency is strongest, Afghanistan's tribes tends to be weakest, so the tribes are not in a position, en masse, to bring the fighting to an end.

And if you go over to the southwest of the country where the worst of the insurgency is going on, you'll find that most of the districts are shared between several tribes, so no one tribe is in a position to deliver security for the whole of the district.
Having sat down and talked to Taliban in Helmand province, which is really the heart - especially these days - of the insurgency, can you see a time when they will be reintegrated into Afghanistan?
I am confident that substantial numbers of people who are currently fighting on the Taliban side are willing to reintegrate.
And if the program, this time, is run better than it has been previously, we'll see significant numbers coming in.
That's not, of course, the same as saying that the conflict's going to come to an end or that everybody is going to be able to take the deal.
Who then, in your opinion, is not likely to take the deal?

I think that the senior commanders and the leadership, there's going to be another level of dialogue with the leadership of the Taliban, which is going to involve - if it works - it's going to involve them coming in, basically, with a - some kind of a share of the government that they will, of course, have to concede the key demands that they decisively break with al-Qaida.
And they who claim that they are still trying to build an ideal Islamic state system inside Afghanistan, but actually they will be absorbed into the political system.
But you believe that could happen.
Well, there's a strong logic to it.
First of all, that they more or less, the only thing the Taliban admits publicly that they are fighting for is the withdrawal of foreign troops.
And that's, on one level, that's quite funny, because I mean every country which has deployed troops to Afghanistan is rather keen to bring them home, you know, but bring them home with the job done.
And the second thing, which everybody assumes that they should be fighting for, is they must be fighting to try and reestablish say the Taliban government over the whole of Afghanistan.
Any sensible Afghan, including people in the Taliban leadership, know that that's never going to happen because even without the U.S.
troops, they would not be strong enough to take over the whole of Afghanistan.
Those Afghans who are inimically opposed to the idea of Taliban rule are frankly too strong.
Michael Semple is a fellow at Harvard's Carr Center for Human Rights Policy.
Thanks for joining us.
You're welcome.
And you are listening to MORNING EDITION from NPR News.
(SOUNDBITE OF SONG, ""WAITING FOR THE SUN"")
(Singing) I was waiting for the sun...
In 1992, this song launched the careers of the Minnesota band The Jayhawks.
They quickly became seminal forces in the burgeoning sound known as alternative country.
Co-founders Mark Olson and Gary Louris found their harmonies and their songwriting styles fit together like few others: a perfect match.
(SOUNDBITE OF SONG, ""WAITING FOR THE SUN"")
(Singing) It was not lost on me.
But by the end of 1995, Mark Olson left the band.
Gary Louris kept the name and the spirit alive with three more Jayhawks albums without Olson, but it never quite sounded like this.
(SOUNDBITE OF SONG, ""SHE WALKS IN SO MANY WAYS"")
(Singing) She walks in so many ways.
She wanders alone in the shade.
She shakes herself in the dust, whispers all things will be silence.
This is ""She Walks in So Many Ways"" from the new Jayhawks album ""Mockingbird Time,"" featuring Gary Louris and Mark Olson for the first time in 16 years.
When I spoke with them yesterday, Mark told me he left the band not because of any animosity but just to find out what else he could do with his life.

I found a - basically an abandoned house in the Mojave Desert and an old man that was very excited about working on it, and I threw myself into that for a year, and then played music with Victoria Williams and the Creekdippers for many years and toured and made albums and went all over Europe.
And that only went on for really seven, eight years, and then Gary and I got back together and started playing music again.
But I think part of the strangeness of the story is that we didn't make an album then for about eight years.
We just kind of went around playing music.
And I don't know if you want to add something to that, Gary.
You know, we'd meet up and play.
We started by playing some Jayhawks songs, and then we said, well, let's play some new stuff.
And then from there, it just got to the point where we listened to the people.
And what they said was, we want to hear the band.
When's the band getting back together?
(SOUNDBITE OF SONG, ""SHE WALKS IN SO MANY WAYS"")
Gary came out to my house in California, and we came up with this song called ""Hey Mr. Man."" I had a cousin there.
He wasn't working at the time, and hadn't worked, and had used the notion that there weren't any jobs anymore as a great base to launch his nonworking career.
(SOUNDBITE OF LAUGHTER)
(SOUNDBITE OF SONG, ""HEY MR. MAN"")
(Singing) Sleeping in the morning light, in the morning light, in the morning light.
All these alibis, thinking I could stay a while and watch the grass grow high.
But of all things, we kind of ganged up on him.
And now, he's actually working for the Merchant Marines.
He went to the academy, and he's on a ship right now, so the song isn't really true anymore.
If you're listening out there.
So if you listen to the song, it's about someone who doesn't want to work.
(SOUNDBITE OF SONG, ""HEY MR. MAN"")
(Singing) Go back to the farm.
My guests are Gary Louris and Mark Olson of the band The Jayhawks.

Their new album is called ""Mockingbird Time."" One of the sort of signature sounds of the Jayhawks is the harmony you two have together - a lot of comparisons to Simon and Garfunkel and the way your voices fit.
Did you find when you came back together that your voices still fit?
Had they changed?
Did they fit in a new way?
It's one of those things that it's a combination of chemistry.
Obviously, we just - even when we talk, you can hear - I hear the high part - my voice is high and Mark's lower - and he hears the low parts.
And something about our tonal quality, when they fit together just right, they resonate and become kind of a third voice.
But the basic blueprint of the two of us and how our voices fit were there from the very beginning.
But it is true that when you two resonate, it does become its own sound.
It is a third thing entirely.
You must be able to hear it and feel it when you hit it.
It's vibration.
That's what harmony is.
